topic: Someone discovers their teenager has a secret social media account
raw_output:
[story 1]

The discovery hit me like a physical blow. I found the login details tucked inside an old, forgotten charging cable. My hands were shaking as I stared at the screen, the carefully constructed facade of parental calm crumbling around the edges. It was a whole other life, a curated version of Maya that existed entirely outside my purview. I felt a cold knot tighten in my stomach, a deep, churning dissatisfaction that had been building for months—the feeling that I was perpetually out of sync with her. When she finally walked in, whistling some irritating pop song, I managed a tight, brittle smile. "Everything alright, sweetie?" The silence that followed was heavy, thick with unspoken knowledge, and I just wanted the floor to swallow me whole.

[story 2]

I watched her scroll through the feed, the glow of the phone illuminating the sharp angles of her profile. Every perfectly filtered picture, every carefully chosen caption, felt like a deliberate act of distance. A sour taste coated my tongue. I’d been trying so hard to connect, to bridge the gap between us, but all I saw was this hidden world she inhabited, one I wasn't privy to. When I finally confronted her, my voice came out unnervingly level, almost too calm. "I saw something," I stated, the words tasting like ash. She didn't look up immediately, her thumb moving with practiced nonchalance. The slight tightening around her eyes was all the confirmation I needed.

[story 3]

The realization settled over me slowly, a heavy blanket draped over my chest. It wasn't anger that surfaced first, but a profound sense of being sidelined. I traced the outline of the account name on the screen with a fingertip, feeling a deep, internal deflation. This secret space, this parallel self she maintained, felt like a betrayal of the trust I thought we shared. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the hardwood floor. "Why didn't you tell me?" The question was sharp, laced with a weariness that had accumulated over years of feeling like an outsider in my own home. Her immediate defensiveness, the quick shift in her posture, only solidified the feeling that this was a battle I was already losing.
